data.title: Foreign private sector holds key to U.S. Treasuries, dollar: McGeever
data.description:
Amid the cacophony of chaos in financial markets created by the Trump administration's tariffs, the loudest - and most alarming - signal is surely the simultaneous slump in the dollar and U.S. Treasury bonds.

It's too early to say whether this is the beginning of a more prolonged trend. But it is a warning that faith in U.S. assets - and indeed, the global financial system of the past half-century shaped in America's image - cannot be taken for granted.

It's never advisable to let long-term forecasts be swayed by short-term price moves but last week was potentially pivotal, both for the dollar and Treasuries.

Whether it turns out that way will be determined to a large extent by overseas private sector investors, who have emerged as significant marginal buyers even as foreign official sector holdings of Treasuries barely moved over the past decade.

Private sector investors have greatly increased their holdings of Treasuries and, by doing so largely on an unhedged basis, also greatly increased their exposure to the dollar.

If a global crisis of confidence in the U.S. does snowball, they will be more likely to head for the exits before their more staid, conservative central bank counterparts.

Foreigners held $8.5 trillion of Treasuries in January, U.S. Treasury figures show - central banks with $3.8 trillion and the private sector with $4.7 trillion. Five years ago central bank holdings were $4.2 trillion and private sector investors held $2.9 trillion, and a decade ago official holdings were more than twice as large as the private sector's stash of $2 trillion.

Japanese institutions and households are among the largest holders of Treasuries on the planet, and if they reflect private sector thinking in other countries, investors and policymakers should brace for further market upheaval.

According to Bank of America, Japanese private investors sold $17.5 billion in long-term Treasuries in the week through April 4, the largest amount of foreign bond sales since before the U.S. election in November.

DOLLAR TIDE TURNING

Eroding confidence in the dollar and Treasuries, the two pillars of the global financial system would of course have serious long-term consequences for the world. The more immediate fallout for investors has been no less dramatic.

Last week the 30-year U.S. Treasury yield rose 48.5 basis points. It was the biggest weekly rise since June 1982. The benchmark 10-year yield's 50-basis point surge was its steepest weekly rise since November 2001.

At the same time, the dollar fell nearly 3% against a basket of major currencies. Excluding the Global Financial Crisis and the COVID-19 pandemic, this has only happened five times in the past 30 years, and one of them was last month.

Analysts at Goldman Sachs note that last week also marked only the fifth week since 1980 where the euro, or a pre-single currency weighted equivalent, rose 2% and the S&P 500 fell 2%. In other words, a significant slump on Wall Street is rarely accompanied by an equally steep decline in the dollar.

Goldman's FX strategy team last week flipped their dollar call to a bearish view, arguing that the recent breakdown in "usual" correlations is a clear sign that "markets are concerned about what recent policy actions imply about U.S. governance and institutional credibility."

They were joined on Monday by strategists at HSBC, who note that "as long as U.S. economic policy uncertainty is elevated, it will be difficult for the dollar to recover versus other core currencies."

Also on Monday, Barclays' FX team published a note, "The end of the dollar as we know it?", in which they observe that the euro's spike to $1.1480 from $1.0950 last week was a move rarely seen over a six-month time frame never mind two days.

The tide is turning against the dollar and U.S. bonds, certainly at the long end of the curve, and the power to direct the flow is now increasingly in private, not official sector hands.

(The opinions expressed here are those of the author, a columnist for Reuters.)

data.title: Ericsson CEO: Recovery in U.S. is Encouraging as It's a Front-Runner Market
European indices mostly higher to start the day
data.description:
Key points:
  • Eurostoxx +0.3%
  • Germany DAX +0.9%
  • France CAC -0.3%
  • UK FTSE +0.4%
  • Spain IBEX +0.6%
  • Italy FTSE MIB +0.6%

The French benchmark index is the laggard as luxury stocks are down, following LVHM's poor Q1 earnings after the close yesterday. The threat of further trade conflict globally isn't going to help with the outlook on that. As for the broader market mood, it is holding up with S&P 500 futures now up 0.2% on the day.

data.title: Romanian Industrial Output Returns to Fall
data.description:
Industrial production in Romania declined by 3.2% year-on-year in February 2025, reversing a 2.1% increase in the previous month.

Output fell for both mining and quarrying (-6.3% vs 2.1% in January) and manufacturing (-3.7% vs 2.7%).

Meanwhile, the output rebounded in electricity, gas, steam, and air conditioning supply (2.5% vs. -3.3%).

On a monthly basis, industrial output dropped 2.1% in February 2025, following a 2.0% increase in the previous month.

data.title: TomTom beats Q1 revenue expectations, shares rise
data.description:
** Shares in TomTom rise 15% as the Dutch digital mapping specialist beats revenue expectations for the first quarter

** It released Q1 revenue of 140.4 million euros ($159.38 million), up 1% year-on-year, and above the 136 million euros expected in a company-provided consensus

** "We believe this is a good overall update which provides comfort after the weaker FY24 update," ING analyst Mark Hesselink says in a note

** The revenue is lead by better-than-expected performance in its automotive location technology business coupled with an 18% growth in its Enterprise location technology business

** "Automotive was a positive surprise given the ongoing weakness in global car markets" Hesselink adds

** Shares in TomTom are on track for their best day since September 2018

($1 = 0.8809 euros)

data.title: Sagarsoft (India) Forms New Subsidiary in US; Shares Jump 9%
data.description:
Technology company Sagarsoft (India) has incorporated a subsidiary in the US named Sarral Global to expand its cyber security business in the country, according to a BSE filing on Tuesday.

IT CATS, a wholly-owned subsidiary of Sagarsoft, holds 51% in the new Amercican subsidiary with an initial investment of $5,100.

Sagarsoft's shares were up over 9% in recent trade.

data.title: Rising Risks Spur Downgrades to China Growth Projections
data.description:
Several investment banks have cut growth projections for China, seeing bigger-than-expected economic risks from U.S. tariffs.

UBS on Tuesday lowered expectations for China's economic growth to 3.4% this year from 4% previously, a far cry from Beijing's annual target of around 5%.

Economists at the Swiss bank anticipate that China's exports to the U.S. will plunge by two-thirds in the coming quarters, with overall exports sliding 10% in dollar terms this year.

In 2026, China's economic growth could slow to just 3%, they said in a report.

Goldman Sachs and Citigroup also cut growth views on China in recent days, citing similar concerns about tariffs announced by the Trump administration.

President Trump's unpredictable policy announcements have clouded outlook for the world's second-largest economy, with Beijing also hitting back with its own tariffs on the U.S.

"Given the extremely large uncertainty around the eventual tariffs related to China and U.S. tariffs on the rest of the world, we approach the estimation of tariff impact with great caution and underscore the unusually high margins of error," the UBS economists said.

Signs of green shoots in China's economy had prompted several growth forecast upgrades in March, but optimism evaporated after the U.S.'s steeper-than-expected tariffs.

Goldman Sachs economists now pencil in China's economic growth at 4% this year, versus 4.5% previously, while Citigroup cut its forecast to 4.2% from 4.7%.

First-quarter GDP numbers due Wednesday are likely to show that China's growth held up well, but part of that could be due to tariff front-running, and analysts widely expect Beijing to ramp up stimulus in the coming months.

Write to Singapore editors at singaporeeditors@dowjones.com

data.title: JOCELYN BENSON, SECRETARY OF STATE, MICHIGAN IS INTERVIEWED ON MSNBC`S INSIDE WITH JEN PSAKI
data.description:
TRANSCRIPT

April 14, 2025

NEWS EVENT

JOCELYN BENSON, SECRETARY OF STATE, MICHIGAN

JOCELYN BENSON, SECRETARY OF STATE, MICHIGAN IS INTERVIEWED ON

MSNBC`S INSIDE WITH JEN PSAKI

VIQ Media Transcription, Inc.

20 East Thomas Road, Suite 2200

Phoenix, AZ 85012

asc.info@viqsolutions.com

Copyright 2025. Provided under license from VIQ Media Transcription, Inc.

All materials herein are protected by United States copyright law

and/or license from VIQ Media Transcription, Inc., and may not be

reproduced, distributed, transmitted, displayed, published or

broadcast without the prior written permission of

VIQ Media Transcription, Inc.

You may not alter or remove any trademark, copyright or other

notice from copies of the content.

JOCELYN BENSON, SECRETARY OF STATE, MICHIGAN IS INTERVIEWED ON

MSNBC`S INSIDE WITH JEN PSAKI

APRIL 14, 2025

SPEAKERS:

JOCELYN BENSON, SECRETARY OF STATE, MICHIGAN

JEN PSAKI, MSNBC HOST

JEN PSAKI, MSNBC HOST: Joining me now though is Michigan Secretary of State Jocelyn Benson. She`s also running for Governor of Michigan in 2026.

It`s great to see you. Thank you for being with me. And we thought about who we wanted to talk to about this and you were kind of the perfect person, so thank you.

I know this story must feel personal for you. I know you`re from Pittsburgh. Last year, your home was also targeted in several swatting attacks. In 2020, dozens of armed protesters gathered outside your home while you and your son were inside. Given that, I just wanted to ask you to help people understand this. What was going through your mind when you heard these details and saw these images from the governor`s residence?

JOCELYN BENSON, SECRETARY OF STATE, MICHIGAN: Well, thanks for having me. You know, I started my career investigating antisemitism and neo-Nazis throughout the country in the late 90s. So, what actually went through my mind first was this was right after Passover the first night. The Passover you know as we still learn more about the attack that context especially in a state where we saw the attack on the Tree of Life Synagogue just a few years earlier to a governor who swore, you know, his office related to, you know, a tome related to the synagogue. That is where my mind went immediately.

And then secondly, my mind went to the family. That this is attack not on a person, not on an ideology only. This is attack on a family, a family that is committed to service of all people, the entire commonwealth. So, it was deeply disturbing, deeply hurtful, and all too reminiscent of what too many of us have endured in this contentious political era over the last several years as hateful rhetoric and hateful threats has been on the rise.

PSAKI: You heard Governor Shapiro say something that is, I mean, absolutely appropriate and so important to hear at this moment. It has to stop. We have to be better than this. What do you think — you`ve investigated these cases, you`ve experienced these threats yourself, what is it actually going to take to change this trend of political violence that so many people are facing?

BENSON: It is going to take all of us coming together, not allowing this hateful moment to divide us, isolate us. I mean, the goal of an administration or a movement or an ideology that tries to divide and create chaos and confusion and hate is to isolate and make people feel alone. This is a moment where we have to actually come together to reunite. Even if we can disagree on things, we can disagree without being disagreeable and hateful.

And so, for me, the reaction is for all of us as American citizens to realize we are indeed better than this. This hateful rhetoric and these threats do not define us. They have never defined this country when we are at our best. And this is a moment for all of us to put ideology even aside and come together and say we are indeed better than this. And we do need to be able to return to that civility, that unity and define who we are as Americans through that lens, not through this lens of division and hateful rhetoric.

PSAKI: I also wanted to ask you about President Trump`s response. I played it because I think it was important for people to see. It isn`t always important for people to see what he has to say but every now and then just to draw the contrast. He didn`t post or say anything about the attack for more than a day. He was asked about it this morning. As you heard earlier, he said in part, "The attacker was not a fan of Trump." But what did you make of the response?

BENSON: I mean, honestly, I felt like he made it about himself when it should have been about the people of Pennsylvania and the governor of the Commonwealth who were immediately threatened by these acts. And this was an opportunity for the president to bring us together to unilaterally condemn this and any other hateful attack and to remind ourselves that we are the United States of America, we can come together in times like these and unify behind and against hateful attacks and threats and rhetoric. This was an opportunity for him to do that and it was — I was disappointed — not surprised, disappointed, and saddened to see him miss that opportunity and instead essentially make it about himself.

PSAKI: Who else would you like to hear from more forcefully right now?

BENSON: I think every single leader in America has to strongly condemn these attacks. I mean, we saw this in Michigan a few years ago when we saw this incredulous kidnapping attempt that was thwarted on Governor Whitmer. What the antidote to moments like this is for leaders and again for all of us to come together condemn these violent threats do not allow them to define who we are, certainly do not allow them to have any sense of normalcy.

And so, for me, every single leader in this country should immediately unequivocally condemn this attack, offer support to Governor Shapiro and his family and the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania because this is an attack not just on him but the entire Commonwealth, and use this as an opportunity to say this is not going to define who we are. Let`s turn the page, come back together as Americans, and chart a course forward where we can agree to disagree without being disagreeable and certainly without going down the path of violence and hateful acts.

PSAKI: Jocelyn Benson, thank you so much. I really appreciate you taking the time. We`ll be right back.

BENSON: Thanks for having me.

END

data.title: Singapore dissolves parliament ahead of imminent election
data.description:
Singapore's parliament was dissolved on Tuesday ahead of a general election to be held within three months, a government gazette said.

The vote will be the first electoral test for Prime Minister Lawrence Wong, who took over from long-time premier Lee Hsien Loong as leader of the People's Action Party in May 2024.

The PAP is almost certain to dominate and win most seats, as it has in every vote since independence in 1965, although its share of the popular vote will be closely watched after one of its worst electoral performances in the last contest in 2020.

The election will take place amid a gloomy economic outlook as U.S. President Donald Trump's tariffs threaten to hit activity in the trade-reliant nation, which on Monday downgraded its growth forecast for 2025 to 0% to 2%, from 1% to 3%.

The ruling party's popularity has dimmed in recent elections as the opposition steadily gained more ground in parliament, winning an unprecedented 6 seats in 2011 and 2015, and 10 in 2020.

The upcoming election will have four more seats compared to the last vote in 2020, with 97 lawmakers elected from 15 single-member electoral divisions and 18 divisions with 4 or 5 members each.

In February, Wong delivered what analysts called "a full-blown election budget" with goodies for all Singaporeans ahead of the polls.

data.title: Ericsson shares jump after Q1 core profit beat, growing US networks sales
data.description:
** Shares in Ericsson rise nearly 6% after the Swedish telecom gear maker booked a better-than-expected quarterly profit as its customers in the U.S. scurried to make orders ahead of tariffs

** Ericsson posts its adjusted EBIT for Q1 at 6.21 billion Swedish crowns ($637 million) against 4.44 billion expected by analysts on average

** It says sales in Americas business rise 20% yoy with strong Networks growth in North America amid increased network investments in anticipation of the tariffs

** Group sales in the quarter miss LSEG-estimates by 1%, though, coming at 55.0 billion crowns with sales shrinking y/y in other markets

** "Overall the results at the company show the focus on profitable businesses helping margin coupled with the cost reductions implemented", J.P. Morgan analysts say

** US strength due to bringing forward of demand due to tariffs is a read across to Nokia , the broker adds

** The Finnish group's shares rise 2% in morning trade

($1 = 9.7511 Swedish crowns)

data.title: Ericsson Shares Should Rise After Earnings BeatMarket Talk
data.description: Ericsson shares should react positively to results that beat estimates across the board, with full-year consensus earnings estimates likely to rise by a low-double-digit percentage, JPMorgan analysts write. The results were driven by strong margin improvements in all its business units, they say. The networks business was very strong, with the gross margin improvement the key driver. A key part of the very strong margin was strength in the U.S. business, at least partially driven by customers buying in anticipation of tariffs. "With tariffs now in place but not substantially impacting Ericsson's business yet, there may be risk of weaker U.S. sales in the second quarter or beyond and this could have implication for future margin." (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

data.title: ProSiebenSat.1 Acquires Remaining 10.1% Stake in Jochen Schweizer Mydays Held by Founder
Japan automakers propel Nikkei after Trump hints at tariff relief
data.description:
Japan's Nikkei share average climbed nearly 1% on Tuesday, propelled by a rally in automakers after U.S. President Donald Trump hinted at granting the sector some kind of tariff reprieve.

The Nikkei ended the day up 0.84% at 34,267.54, with the broader Topix adding 1%.

Toyota Motor jumped 3.7%, lifting the Tokyo Stock Exchange's auto and parts maker index by 3.6% - making it the standout performer among the bourse's 33 industry groupings. Tire makers (.IRUBR.T) followed with a 2.5% advance, ranking second.

Speaking on Monday at the White House, Trump said he was considering a modification to the 25% tariffs imposed on foreign auto and auto parts imports from Mexico, Canada and other places. Those tariffs could raise the costs of a car by thousands of dollars, and Trump said car companies "need a little bit of time because they're going to make 'em here."

Despite concerns over potential trade policy reversals from Trump, the Nikkei's second straight day of about 1% gains provides some respite for a market that was whipsawed last week by alternating days of sharp sell-offs and rebounds of up to 9%.

"For the first time in a while, trading is back to what you would call normal ranges," said Kazuo Kamitani, a strategist at Nomura Securities.

At the same time he cautioned that market moves may pick up again with earnings season in Japan and the United States gathering pace this week.

Japan and the U.S. are also due to begin trade talks on Thursday in Washington.

"Trump says the aim is to get factories moved to the United States," said Kamitani. "Is that market friendly? I would say no."

data.title: ProSiebenSat.1 Exercised Purchase Option to Transfer Founder's Remaining Stake
Prostatype Genomics Plans Rights Issue to Fund US, Europe Commercial Activities
data.description:
Prostatype Genomics plans a rights issue of units with preferential rights for existing shareholders to support its commercial activities in the US and Europe.

The Swedish prognostic gene test manufacturer plans to issue a maximum of 6,835,213 units, with each unit consisting of four new shares and three TO5 warrants priced at 4 kronor apiece, according to a Tuesday release. The planned fundraising is subject to approval at the company's annual general meeting on May 15.

Full capitalization is expected to fund the company's operations until at least the third quarter of 2026. The proceeds are intended to fund the completion of a US validation study and Medicare approval process for the Prostatype gene test, support ongoing commercialization in the US and Europe, and repay a bridge loan.

If fully subscribed, Prostatype Genomics could initially receive 27.3 million kronor before costs from the sale of the new shares within the units. Meanwhile, if all warrants are exercised, the company could receive an additional maximum of 30.8 million kronor before costs.

data.title: South Korean Stocks Extend Gains on Auto Boost
data.description:
The benchmark KOSPI rose 0.9% to close at 2,477 points on Tuesday, extending gains from the previous session, with auto stocks leading the rally.

This came after US President Trump hinted at a potential pause in auto tariffs, providing additional relief to the market after the suspension of levies on certain consumer electronics.

Among index heavyweights, automakers Hyundai Motor (4.3%) and its sister company Kia Corp (3.7%) led the charge.

Additionally, chipmakers, including Samsung Electronics (0.8%) and SK Hynix (0.6%), surged after South Korea announced a $23 billion support package for its critical chip sector amid rising US tariff uncertainty.

Meanwhile, drugmakers, such as Samsung Biologics (-0.6%) and Celltrion (-2.3%), declined as the US advanced its plans to impose tariffs on pharmaceutical imports through trade probes.

Investors now await the Bank of Korea’s monetary policy decision on Thursday, which is expected to keep the key rate unchanged.

data.title: ProSiebenSat.1 Says It Gains Flexibility for Future Direction of Business With Full Ownership
Asian Stock Markets Rise Amid Hopes for More U.S. Tariff ExemptionsUpdate
data.description:
By Dow Jones Newswires Staff

Asian stock markets rose Tuesday, with shares of major automakers gaining amid hopes for more U.S. tariff exemptions that could ease the impact of tougher trade barriers.

Japan's Nikkei Stock Average rose 0.8%, with Toyota Motor adding 3.7% and Honda Motor gaining 3.6% after President Trump mentioned potential tariff exemptions for carmakers.

South Korea's Kospi climbed 0.9% with carmakers Hyundai Motor and Kia up 4.3% and 3.4%, respectively.

President Trump on Monday said he is considering some short-term exemptions to the 25% tariff on foreign-made vehicle imports.

"I'm looking at something to help some of the car companies," he told reporters, without elaborating further.

Trump's remarks came after the administration announced that smartphones, computers, memory chips and several other categories of products will be exempt from reciprocal tariffs, but that other sector-specific levies may be announced.

Singapore's Straits Times Index was last up 1.9%, and Malaysia's benchmark Kuala Lumpur Composite Index was 0.25% higher.

Sentiment was shakier in China and Hong Kong, with benchmark indexes reversing opening gains to sit lower in afternoon trade. The benchmark Shanghai Composite Index was down 0.2%, while Hong Kong's Hang Seng Index was 0.2% lower and the Nasdaq-like Hang Seng Tech Index was off by 1.4%.

Crude-oil futures also rose on hopes for more U.S. tariff exemptions.

The yen was weaker against most other Group of 10 and Asian currencies in morning trade amid appetite for risky assets.

Risk appetite has returned, two members of Sucden Financial's Research team said in a research report. However, they added that "despite the temporary reprieve, market uncertainty remains elevated, with Trump warning the tariff exemption could be short-lived."

Write to Ronnie Harui at ronnie.harui@wsj.com

data.title: BioPorto Completes DKK34 Million Equity Raise
data.description:
BioPorto completed a private placement of 25 million new shares and raised gross proceeds of 33.5 million Danish kroner.

The in vitro diagnostics company expects the new shares to be admitted to trading and official listing by April 30, according to a Tuesday release.

BioPorto said the proceeds will help finance new clinical trials to seek clearance from the US Food and Drug Administration for ProNephro AKI NGAL for adult use in the US.

data.title: Gorilla Gold's Upcoming Project Drilling Seen as Biggest Catalyst, Euroz Hartleys Says
data.description:
Gorilla Gold Mines upcoming drilling program at its Lakeview prospect within its Comet Vale project in Western Australia is the biggest catalyst for the company, according to a Tuesday note by Euroz Hartleys.

On Tuesday, the company reported its maiden gold estimate at its Vivien Gold project of 2.1 million tonnes at 4.1 grams per tonne of gold, amounting to 278,000 ounces.

However, Euroz noted the market is currently most focused on drilling activity at the Comet Vale and Mulwarrie projects.

Shares of the company rose past 2% at market close

data.title: Chinese Shares Close Mixed Amid U.S.-China Trade UncertaintiesMarket Talk
data.description: Chinese shares closed mixed, with the benchmark Shanghai Composite Index edging up 0.15% at 3267.66. The Shenzhen Composite Index shed 0.2% and the ChiNext Price Index was down 0.1%. Investors watched for further trade-related developments between the U.S. and China as well as potential stimulus measures out of Beijing. Pharmaceutical and bank stocks led gains, with Sichuan Biokin Pharmaceutical rising 7.9% and China Resources Sanjiu Medical & Pharmaceutical up 3.0%. Bank of Chengdu and Hua Xia Bank gained 2.8% and 2.2%, respectively. Among decliners, Wanhua Chemical Group was down 5.4% and China Tourism Group Duty Free lost 3.5%. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

data.title: PRS REIT's Fiscal Q3 Home Completions Rise
data.description:
PRS REIT said Tuesday that home completions and estimated rental value increased year over year for the third quarter of fiscal 2025.

The real estate investment trust's home completions for the three months ended March 31, were 5,443, compared with 5,308 completed homes previously.

Meanwhile, its estimated rental value per year was 69.6 million pounds sterling, compared with 62.3 million pounds.

PRS REIT said that it will declare its fiscal third quarter dividend in May.

data.title: LVMH shares slump after luxury giant's Q1 sales miss forecast
data.description:
LVMH shares slid lower on Tuesday after the world's largest luxury group posted first quarter revenues that undershot expectations.

LVMH shares were down 5.2 percent in early session trading, dragging down the shares of rival luxury goods companies such as Kering and Hermes .

On Monday, LVMH reported a 3% decline in first-quarter sales - well below analyst expectations for 2% growth - which gave a first sign that luxury companies could face another tough year following President Donald Trump's recent tariff announcements, which sparked fears of a recession.

data.title: Inhalation Sciences Sweden Bags Equipment Order from Pharma Client
data.description:
Inhalation Sciences Sweden (ISAB.ST) secured an order worth 41,000 euros from an unnamed recurring pharmaceutical customer.

The order covers the lab equipment developer's advanced inhalation research equipment and instruments, according to a Tuesday release.

data.title: Kuwait's state-owned KNPC and KIPIC start steps to merge
data.description: State-owned Kuwait Petroleum Corporation (KPC) on Tuesday began the executive steps to merge with another state-owned firm, the Integrated Petroleum Industries Company (KIPIC), state news agency KUNA cited KIPIC CEO as saying.

data.title: Ericsson CEO: Actions Already Taken Position Co to Perform Across Different Market Conditions
